body_text:
Section 5.2 Definite Integrals of Trigonometric Functions

EXAMPLE 2 Compute the definite integral (Figure 4)
$$
\int_{0}^{2 \pi} \frac{\cos 2 \theta}{2+\cos \theta} d \theta
$$

Solution Use (3), (4), and (5) with $n=2$, and get
$$
\int_{0}^{2 \pi} \frac{\cos 2 \theta}{2+\cos \theta} d \theta=-i \int_{C_{1}(0)} \frac{\frac{1}{2}\left(z^{2}+\frac{1}{z^{2}}\right)}{2+\frac{1}{2}\left(z+\frac{1}{z}\right)} \frac{d z}{z}=-i \int_{C_{1}(0)} \frac{z^{4}+1}{z^{2}\left(z^{2}+4 z+1\right)} d
$$

We now compute the last integral using residues. We clearly have a pole of ord at 0 . To compute the residue at 0 , we use Theorem 2, Section 5.1, with $m= z_{0}=0$, and get
$$
\begin{aligned}
\operatorname{Res}\left(\frac{z^{4}+1}{z^{2}\left(z^{2}+4 z+1\right)}, 0\right) & =\lim _{z \rightarrow 0} \frac{d}{d z} \frac{z^{4}+1}{z^{2}+4 z+1} \\
& =\lim _{z \rightarrow 0} \frac{3 z^{3}\left(z^{2}+4 z+1\right)-\left(z^{4}+1\right)(2 z+4)}{\left(z^{2}+4 z+1\right)^{2}}=
\end{aligned}
$$

The roots of $z^{2}+4 z+1=0$ are $z_{1}=-2-\sqrt{3} \approx-3.7$ and $z_{2}=-2+\sqrt{3} \approx-0$ (Figure 5). Only $z_{2}$ is inside the unit disk. Since $z_{2}$ is a simple pole, we can comp the residues at $z_{2}$ using Proposition 1(ii), Section 5.1:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\operatorname{Res}\left(\frac{z^{4}+1}{z^{2}} \frac{1}{z^{2}+4 z+1},-2+\sqrt{3}\right) & =\frac{(-2+\sqrt{3})^{4}+1}{(-2+\sqrt{3})^{2}} \frac{1}{\left.\frac{d}{d z}\left(z^{2}+4 z+1\right)\right|_{-2+}} \\
& =\frac{(-2+\sqrt{3})^{4}+1}{(-2+\sqrt{3})^{2}(2 \sqrt{3})}=\frac{7}{\sqrt{3}}
\end{aligned}
$$

1 poles
in Ex-

By the residue theorem, we conclude that
$$
\int_{C_{1}(0)} \frac{z^{4}+1}{z^{2}\left(z^{2}+4 z+1\right)} d z=2 \pi i\left(-4+\frac{7}{\sqrt{3}}\right)
$$
and so
$$
\int_{0}^{2 \pi} \frac{\cos 2 \theta}{2+\cos \theta} d \theta=-i \int_{C_{1}(0)} \frac{z^{4}+1}{z^{2}\left(z^{2}+4 z+1\right)} d z=2 \pi\left(-4+\frac{7}{\sqrt{3}}\right) \approx 0.26
$$

In the preceding examples, we needed the interval of integration to $[0,2 \pi]$, in order for $z=e^{i \theta}$ to trace the whole circle $C_{1}(0)$. Integrals o the interval $[0, \pi]$ can be handled if the integrand $f(\theta)$ is even, since in $t$ case
$$
\int_{0}^{\pi} f(\theta) d \theta=\frac{1}{2} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} f(\theta) d \theta=\frac{1}{2} \int_{0}^{2 \pi} f(\theta) d \theta
$$

The first equality follows because the integrand is even and the second follows because the integrand is $2 \pi$-periodic, and so the integral does
